text:
Abstract

According to the latest IndexBox report on the global Fischer-Tropsch Synthesis Reactors market, the market enters 2026 with broader demand fundamentals, more disciplined procurement behavior, and a more regionally diversified supply architecture.

The global market for Fischer-Tropsch Synthesis Reactors is entering a transformative phase from 2026 to 2035, pivoting from its traditional foundation in large-scale gas-to-liquids (GTL) and coal-to-liquids (CTL) projects toward a more diversified portfolio driven by decarbonization imperatives. This shift is fundamentally supported by tightening global mandates for sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) and marine biofuels, which create a robust demand pull for reactors capable of processing biomass and waste-derived syngas. While capital intensity and volatile feedstock economics remain persistent challenges, technological advancements in modular, scalable reactor designs—particularly slurry phase and microchannel configurations—are lowering entry barriers for smaller-scale, distributed synthetic fuel production. The forecast period will see the commercialization of integrated biorefineries and power-to-liquid (PtL) facilities, where FT reactors act as the critical nexus for converting green hydrogen and captured carbon into drop-in hydrocarbons. This analysis projects market evolution across key end-use segments and geographies, providing a strategic lens on the competitive dynamics between established technology licensors and new entrants aiming to capitalize on the emerging low-carbon fuel economy.

The baseline scenario for the Fischer-Tropsch Synthesis Reactors market from 2026-2035 anticipates moderate but accelerating growth, transitioning from a niche, project-driven industry to a more standardized technology segment within the broader renewable fuels and chemicals value chain. The market's foundation remains the periodic deployment of large-scale reactors for mega GTL projects, primarily in gas-rich regions seeking to monetize stranded resources. However, the core growth engine through 2035 will be the sequential scaling of biomass-to-liquids (BTL) and waste-to-fuels applications, driven by binding policy targets in Europe, North America, and parts of Asia-Pacific. This scenario assumes continued, though not radical, improvements in reactor efficiency and catalyst lifetimes, alongside a gradual reduction in capital expenditure per barrel of capacity for modular units. It also incorporates the realistic constraint of high upfront costs and long project lead times, which will temper explosive growth but not prevent a steady increase in the pipeline of announced and financed projects. The competitive landscape is expected to evolve, with increased collaboration between reactor technology providers, energy majors, and waste management firms to develop integrated solutions. Market value will increasingly derive not just from the reactor vessel itself, but from the associated process know-how, catalyst management systems, and lifecycle performance guarantees required for bankable sustainable fuel projects.

Demand Drivers and Constraints

Primary Demand Drivers

Stringent global and regional mandates for sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) blending

Policy support and subsidies for advanced biofuels and circular economy projects

Technological advancements enabling smaller-scale, modular FT reactor deployments

Strategic energy security initiatives promoting domestic synthetic fuel production from coal and gas

Growing corporate decarbonization targets creating demand for low-carbon drop-in fuels

Valorization of low-value or stranded carbon resources (associated gas, biomass residues, municipal waste)

Potential Growth Constraints

Exceptionally high capital intensity and long payback periods for FT complexes

Volatile and often unfavorable economics compared to conventional petroleum refining, especially in low oil-price environments

Complexity of feedstock handling and syngas conditioning, particularly for heterogeneous wastes and biomass

Significant water consumption and heat management challenges associated with the highly exothermic FT reaction

Regulatory uncertainty and evolving sustainability certification frameworks for synthetic fuels

Demand Structure by End-Use Industry

Gas-to-Liquids (GTL) Plants (estimated share: 45%)

GTL represents the largest and most established end-use for FT reactors, centered on converting stranded or associated natural gas into high-quality diesel, naphtha, and waxes. Current demand is project-driven, dominated by a handful of mega-facilities in Qatar, the US, and Nigeria. Through 2035, the segment will evolve. While new mega-project announcements will be rare due to capital constraints, demand will shift toward smaller-scale, modular GTL units deployed for gas flaring reduction, offshore associated gas monetization, and regional fuel security. Key demand-side indicators include natural gas price differentials to oil (the 'gas-to-oil spread'), flaring regulation stringency, and national energy security policies. The mechanism driving reactor demand in this segment is the need for robust, skid-mounted designs that can operate reliably in remote or offshore environments, with a focus on maximizing liquid yield from variable gas feedstocks. Current trend: Mature but strategic, with growth in modular deployments.

Major trends: Shift from world-scale to modular, pre-fabricated reactor trains, Integration with floating production storage and offloading (FPSO) vessels for offshore gas, Focus on reactor designs optimized for variable feed gas composition, Retrofitting and capacity expansion projects at existing GTL facilities, and Increased use of GTL products as blending components for cleaner-burning diesel.

Representative participants: Shell, Sasol, Petronas, Chevron, CompactGTL, and Velocys.

Biomass-to-Liquids (BTL) / Waste-to-Fuels (estimated share: 25%)

This segment is the primary growth frontier for FT reactor demand through 2035, propelled by binding SAF and renewable fuel mandates in Europe (ReFuelEU), the US (Inflation Reduction Act), and elsewhere. The current market consists of pilot and first-of-a-kind commercial plants converting forestry residues, agricultural waste, or municipal solid waste (MSW) into synthetic crude. The transition to 2035 involves scaling these technologies to commercial viability. Demand will be driven by the need for reactors specifically engineered to handle the challenges of biomass-derived syngas: tolerance to contaminants (tars, alkalis), flexibility to process varying feedstock mixes, and efficient heat removal due to different syngas compositions. Key indicators are policy-driven SAF premium prices, availability and cost of sustainable biomass feedstocks, and progress in gasification technology. The reactor's role is critical as the gas-cleaned syngas conversion step, where yield and selectivity directly impact project economics. Current trend: High-growth segment driven by sustainability policies.

Major trends: Designs optimized for lower H2:CO ratio syngas from biomass gasification, Integration with advanced gas cleaning systems to protect catalyst life, Development of reactors for co-processing syngas with green hydrogen (PtL integration), Focus on CAPEX reduction through standardization and modularization, and Projects increasingly colocated with existing industrial sites (pulp mills, waste facilities).

Representative participants: Velocys, Fulcrum BioEnergy, Red Rock Biofuels, Enerkem, Topsoe, and Gasum.

Coal-to-Liquids (CTL) Plants (estimated share: 18%)

CTL demand for FT reactors is highly regional, concentrated in countries with abundant coal reserves and strategic imperatives for liquid fuel self-sufficiency, primarily China and South Africa. Current activity involves capacity expansions and efficiency upgrades at existing facilities. Looking to 2035, new greenfield CTL projects in these regions are possible but will face increasing headwinds from carbon emissions concerns. Demand will therefore focus on reactor retrofits and technology upgrades for existing plants aimed at improving yield, reducing energy intensity, and potentially integrating carbon capture. The key demand mechanism is national energy security policy rather than pure economics. Indicators include domestic oil import dependency, government strategic fuel reserve targets, and the political weight of coal-producing regions. Reactors for CTL must be exceptionally robust, often designed for high-sulfur syngas and high wax production, with multi-tubular fixed-bed designs remaining prevalent for their reliability in large-scale operations. Current trend: Geographically concentrated and strategically motivated.

Major trends: Retrofit projects to improve thermal efficiency and catalyst utilization, Exploration of CTL with carbon capture, utilization, and storage (CCUS) integration, Research into direct coal liquefaction hybrids incorporating FT synthesis, Focus on producing high-value chemicals alongside fuels to improve economics, and Technology export from China to other coal-rich nations under Belt and Road initiatives.

Representative participants: Sasol, Shenhua Group (CHN Energy), Yankuang Group, Linc Energy, and INTEG Group.

Synthetic Lubricant & Chemical Feedstock Production (estimated share: 8%)

This segment utilizes FT synthesis primarily to produce high-purity alpha-olefins, paraffinic waxes, and base oils for synthetic lubricants, bypassing the fuel market. Current demand is served by dedicated reactor trains within larger GTL complexes or smaller, specialized facilities. Through 2035, growth will be driven by the premium performance characteristics of FT-derived products (e.g., high viscosity index, purity) in demanding automotive, industrial, and food-grade applications. The demand mechanism is quality-driven and less sensitive to oil price fluctuations than fuel production. Key indicators include automotive OEM specifications for low-evaporation lubricants, growth in high-performance manufacturing, and demand for sustainable chemical feedstocks. Reactors for this segment are optimized for specific product slates—often using fixed-bed designs for precise temperature control—to maximize yield of target carbon number fractions (C6-C10 for olefins, C20+ for waxes). Current trend: High-value, niche application with stable growth.

Major trends: Tightening product specifications driving demand for ultra-pure FT intermediates, Integration of FT reactors with selective oligomerization and hydroprocessing units, Growing demand for bio-based synthetic waxes and oils from BTL pathways, Development of catalyst systems for enhanced alpha-olefin selectivity, and Expansion into new chemical derivatives (detergent alcohols, plasticizers).

Representative participants: Shell, Sasol, ExxonMobil, INEOS, Neste, and BASF.

Power-to-Liquids (PtL) / E-Fuels (estimated share: 4%)

PtL represents the most nascent but potentially transformative end-use, combining green hydrogen (from electrolysis) with captured CO2 to produce synthetic hydrocarbons. As of 2026, this segment is in the pilot and demonstration phase. The pathway to 2035 involves scaling to first commercial plants, driven by policies targeting hard-to-electrify transport sectors (aviation, shipping). Demand for FT reactors here is unique: they must be optimized for a syngas feed with a near-stoichiometric H2:CO ratio of 2:1, derived from CO2 hydrogenation. The key demand mechanism is the future cost trajectory of green hydrogen and the development of scalable CO2 capture from air or point sources. Indicators include renewable electricity costs, electrolyzer capacity deployment, and carbon credit markets. Reactor demand will favor designs with high turndown ratios and rapid response to intermittent hydrogen production, making microchannel and intensified reactor designs particularly relevant. Current trend: Emerging segment with significant long-term potential.

Major trends: Integration of FT reactors with high-temperature co-electrolysis (HTE) systems, Designs focused on dynamic operation to accommodate intermittent renewable power, Intense R&D on catalysts optimized for CO2-derived syngas, Development of fully integrated, containerized PtL modules, and Strategic partnerships between reactor tech firms, electrolyzer manufacturers, and energy companies.

Representative participants: Topsoe, Sunfire GmbH, INERATEC, Carbon Recycling International, Haldor Topsoe, and Air Liquide.

Key Market Participants

Regional Dynamics

Asia-Pacific (estimated share: 38%)

Asia-Pacific holds the largest market share, anchored by China's ongoing strategic investments in coal-to-liquids for energy security and its ambitions in waste-to-energy. China's reactor demand will focus on CTL upgrades and new waste/BTL pilots. Southeast Asia shows potential for modular GTL using associated gas. Japan and South Korea are investing in PtL research and early demonstrations, positioning for future e-fuel imports and technology exports. Direction: Dominant and growing, led by strategic CTL and emerging BTL.

North America (estimated share: 25%)

North America is a key market, combining established GTL capacity (US Gulf Coast) with aggressive policy-driven growth in sustainable fuels. The US, via the Inflation Reduction Act, is catalyzing investments in BTL and waste-to-fuels projects, creating demand for modular reactors. Canada shows interest in BTL using forestry residues. The region's strength in modular engineering and venture capital supports innovative reactor developers. Direction: Steady growth driven by policy and gas resources.

Europe (estimated share: 22%)

Europe is the epicenter of regulatory-driven demand, with binding ReFuelEU and RED III targets mandating synthetic fuel adoption. This creates a strong, near-term pipeline for BTL reactors using waste wood and MSW, particularly in the Nordics and Germany. Europe is also the global leader in PtL pilot projects, with significant R&D funding flowing into integrated reactor-electrolyzer systems for e-kerosene production. Direction: Policy-led transformation toward BTL and PtL.

Middle East & Africa (estimated share: 12%)

This region is home to the world's largest GTL complexes (Qatar, Nigeria), representing a mature but maintenance- and upgrade-driven market. Future reactor demand may come from expansions or debottlenecking projects. There is emerging interest in applying GTL technology to reduce gas flaring. South Africa remains a CTL stronghold, with potential for efficiency-focused reactor retrofits at Sasol's Secunda complex. Direction: Mature GTL base with emerging diversification.

Latin America (estimated share: 3%)

Latin America presents niche opportunities, primarily focused on modular GTL for associated gas in Brazil's pre-salt fields and Argentina's Vaca Muerta formation. Brazil's established biofuels industry also creates a potential pathway for integrated BTL projects using sugarcane residues. The market is fragmented and project-specific, with growth dependent on overcoming investment hurdles and developing local supply chains. Direction: Niche opportunities in associated gas and biofuels.

Market Outlook (2026-2035)

In the baseline scenario, IndexBox estimates a 6.2% compound annual growth rate for the global fischer-tropsch synthesis reactors market over 2026-2035, bringing the market index to roughly 185 by 2035 (2025=100).

Note: indexed curves are used to compare medium-term scenario trajectories when full absolute volumes are not publicly disclosed.

description:

text:
New US-based airline, Magnifica Air, unveiled the first of four aircraft to join its fleet. Set to begin operations in 2027, the Airbus ACJ321neo will be the first aircraft in the fleet that will be exclusively for the airline’s “Private Class” cabin category for launch service.

What is Magnifica Air?

Magnifica Air is a Florida-based luxury airline start-up that was founded in 2025 by Wade Black and Stacey Carey. Operations are set to launch in 2027 and it will offer “private jet-style” travel on scheduled routes between major cities such as New York, Miami, and Los Angeles. South Florida to New York will be the inaugural route.

Stacey Carey, co-founder, stated:

“When you walk onto our planes, they will feel like a private jet,”

The “Private Class” service, will feature a high-end experience with 45–54 seats, including 2–4 private suites, a bar area, and lie-flat leather seats. It will also feature private terminal access, 30-minute pre-departure arrival, and it specialised in-cabin wellness.

The airline is using two Pratt & Whitney PW1133 geared turbofan engines that are capable of running on Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF). This engine series has improved fuel efficiency, reduced noise footprint, and lower emissions compared with previous-generation narrowbodies.

The Fleet and Operations

At Orlando International Airport, Magnifica Air’s new livery was revealed. The first of four aircraft was revealed to be Airbus ACJ321neo which is configured to carry 54 passengers. The aircraft are currently undergoing certification and a 10-month interior completion phase, during which the new Private Class cabins will be fitted.

Kevin Ketterer, vice president of Technical Operations at Magnifica Air, stated:

“The arrival of our first ACJ321neo is a defining operational milestone. It enables the launch of our maintenance and reliability programs, integrating predictive technologies and dedicated support services designed to deliver the level of consistency expected from a new cabin category.”

Magnifica Air has previously outlined plans for a fleet of four aircraft for launch, with additional Airbus A220 and A321neo variants. These aircraft are expected to follow as part of the airline’s broader growth strategy.

The airline aims to provide a “fully private”, seamless experience that is more accessible than full private jet charter, and its goal is to operate some of the greenest jets in the segment.

text:
thetatvaindia

Verified

Edited•48m

Cooking oil is often discarded after being used for frying at home or in restaurants. However, Panipat refinery of IndianOil has now won a certification to use the same oil to produce sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), the company chairman Arvinder Singh Sahney said.

SAF is an alternative fuel made from non-petroleum feedstocks that reduces emissions from air transportation. It can be blended up to 50 per cent in conventional aviation turbine fuel (ATF or jet fuel), depending on availability. India has mandated 1 per cent SAF blending in jet fuel sold to international airlines from 2027.

IndianOil’s Panipat refinery in Haryana has won the International Civil Aviation Organization’s (ICAO) ISCC CORSIA certification (International Sustainability and Carbon Certification - ISCC - developed under the Carbon Offsetting and Reduction Scheme for International Aviation (CORSIA) to produce SAF from used cooking oil, he said.

“We are the only company in the country to get this certification,” he said.

“The refinery will start producing some 35,000 tonnes per annum of SAF from the end of the calendar year.”

The production will be enough to meet the mandated 1 per cent blending requirement for the country in 2027, he added.

Explaining the process, agencies will collect used cooking oil from large users, such as hotel chains, restaurants, and snacks and sweets producing companies like Haldiram, and supply it to Panipat refinery.

Panipat refinery will use this oil to produce SAF, he said.

title: India's bioenergy gains momentum post E20 mandate – Optimism builds for the future
description:
India needs a 2.0 National Ethanol Blending Policy.
The Next Frontier in Aviation: Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF).
The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) has adopted a market-based measure Carbon Offsetting Reduction Scheme for International Aviation (CORSIA), to reduce carbon emissions from international aviation.
SAF is India’s chance to take the lead globally, making use of its agricultural prowess, distillation capacity and low-carbon aspirations.
A study done by Deloitte and ISMA released a report on 11th September 2025, on Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) titled ‘India’s SAF Roadmap’.
text:
In an important policy announcement, the government has mandated the sale of E20 petrol with Research Octane Number (RON) 95 across the entire country.

For the sugar and bioenergy sector, which has been requesting a stable and long-term demand and supply policy, this announcement has landed as a blessing. It has rejuvenated the industry, as it finds a new purpose.

Amongst the initial takers of the decision, Samir Somaiya, Chairman & Managing Director of Godavari Biorefineries Limited, celebrated it as a “very positive development,” highlighting how India is ahead in its journey towards energy transition.

I feel his optimism is infectious.

Industry leaders, from sugar mills to grain-based distillers to advanced biofuel innovators, have been worried for months that expanded ethanol capacities would translate into less offtake. They worried that what if the market is unable to handle the supply?

That feeling has been decisively turned this week by the E20-RON95 mandate.

The E20 mandate, thus far in effect, frees up significant demand, reinforcing price certainty, increasing utilisation and making it easier for producers to plan investments in infrastructure with certainty.

What’s next? Is a question on everyone’s mind.

India boasts of the largest population. As per some estimates, the total energy consumption will almost double by 2045.

Road, aviation, and agriculture will make up a vast part of that consumption. Meeting this growing demand in a sustainable manner in India requires an extensive ethanol roadmap beyond E20.

Countries such as Brazil have long adopted E27 throughout their entire nation.

India needs a 2.0 National Ethanol Blending Policy. Clear timelines for E22, E27 and E30 adoptions. Producers contract on a long-term procurement basis and a technology roadmap for high-blend vehicles, for which Brazil is ready for knowledge transfer and partnership.

Adoption of higher blends will further reduce dependence on crude oil imports and create a reliable new source of income for farmers.

However, the current GST regime and differential duty slabs restrict market growth. A unified lower GST across biofuels and incentives for blending infrastructure at fuel stations would speed up the uptake of their use.

Flex-fuel vehicles (FFVs) need to be mainstream. A national FFV roadmap, including GST cuts, consumer incentives, and fleet-level measures, will enable the full unlocking of E27–E30 in India.

The Next Frontier in Aviation: Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF).

The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) has adopted a market-based measure Carbon Offsetting Reduction Scheme for International Aviation (CORSIA), to reduce carbon emissions from international aviation. India, being a Member State of the ICAO, is under an obligation to comply with the mandatory phase of CORSIA from 2027. Under the CORSIA scheme, airlines are required to offset their emissions above a set baseline of 2019. The CORSIA scheme is applicable for international flights only.

To facilitate the Indian aviation industry’s transition towards carbon-neutral growth, India has joined ICAO’s Assistance, Capacity-building and Training for Sustainable Aviation Fuels (ACT-SAF) programme, which aims to support the mitigation of CO2 emissions from international civil aviation in ICAO Member States. India is also an active member of various working groups of ICAO Committee on Aviation Environment Protection where experts from all member States participate and contribute to the development of environmental standards and guidance material for the international aviation sector.

The Government has approved indicative blending targets of 1% by 2027, 2% by 2028 and 5% by 2030 for SAF in Traditional Aviation Fuel, initially for international flights. Public sector Oil Marketing Companies are actively engaged in achieving the stated blending targets.

A feasible SAF ecosystem in India will require a mechanism for financial viability gap funding, scheduled blending targets on domestic carriers, logistics, etc.

SAF is India’s chance to take the lead globally, making use of its agricultural prowess, distillation capacity and low-carbon aspirations.

A study done by Deloitte and ISMA released a report on 11th September 2025, on Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) titled ‘India’s SAF Roadmap’.

The report said that India’s robust sugar industry has the ability to produce 350 to 400 Cr litres of SAF via surplus ethanol from the 1G route alone by 2040. “The sector holds the potential to meet over ~50% of India’s total SAF demand by 2040. In the near term, the report projects that the sugar sector can easily support 125 to 150 Cr litres of SAF production in 2030 without any disruptions in the ongoing sugar supply or ongoing ethanol blending,” the report outlined.

Isobutanol, produced from the same feedstocks as molasses-based ethanol, has various applications. Its blending with diesel has the potential to produce cleaner, lower-carbon diesel fuels and helps broaden the green fuel portfolio. Isobutanol-sourced bitumen enhancers can ease carbon intensity and increase the durability of roads, providing an eco-friendly route for the high-performance highway construction of India.

With the right policy architecture, biofuels are not just going to power vehicles; they are going to drive India’s economic revolution, delivering cleaner mobility, increased farmer incomes, lower oil imports, and a strong low-carbon future.

The industry is energised, optimistic and poised. It is now time to turn this optimism into action.

text:
Abstract

According to the latest IndexBox report on the global Polyacrylonitrile Precursors market, the market enters 2026 with broader demand fundamentals, more disciplined procurement behavior, and a more regionally diversified supply architecture.

The global Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) Precursors market, the essential raw material for over 90% of the world's carbon fiber, is entering a decade of accelerated transformation from 2026 to 2035. This analysis projects robust growth driven by the relentless expansion of lightweight, high-performance carbon fiber composites across strategic industries. While traditional sectors like aerospace remain critical, the forecast horizon will be defined by the mass adoption of composites in wind energy, hydrogen storage, and automotive lightweighting as part of global decarbonization efforts. The market's trajectory is intrinsically linked to carbon fiber demand, creating a scenario where precursor production capacity and technological advancement become pivotal bottlenecks. This report details the baseline market outlook, identifying key demand drivers from policy-led energy transitions and supply-side restraints stemming from capital intensity and raw material volatility. We analyze the evolving competitive landscape, regional shifts in production and consumption, and the specific demand mechanisms within each major end-use sector that will shape investment and strategy through 2035.

The baseline scenario for the Polyacrylonitrile Precursors market from 2026 to 2035 is one of sustained, above-GDP growth, underpinned by the structural shift toward advanced composite materials. The market's fundamental driver is the carbon fiber industry, which consumes the majority of high-quality PAN precursor. Growth will be non-linear, with periods of accelerated expansion tied to new capacity rollouts in downstream carbon fiber and composite part manufacturing. The scenario assumes continued supportive regulatory policies for renewable energy and vehicle emissions, which directly stimulate demand for wind turbine blades and lightweight automotive components. Geopolitical factors affecting acrylonitrile monomer supply and trade flows for finished precursors introduce a layer of volatility. Technologically, the baseline expects gradual improvements in precursor stabilization and carbonization yields, enhancing efficiency but requiring significant R&D investment. Market structure will remain consolidated among a few global players, though new entrants may emerge in Asia-Pacific, attracted by regional demand growth. Price dynamics will be influenced by acrylonitrile cost, energy prices, and the premium for specialized grades required for aerospace and pressure vessel applications.

Demand Drivers and Constraints

Primary Demand Drivers

Accelerated deployment of wind energy requiring longer, lighter composite blades

Automotive lightweighting mandates to reduce emissions, boosting carbon fiber reinforced plastic (CFRP) use

Expansion of hydrogen economy driving demand for Type IV composite pressure vessels

Sustained demand from commercial aerospace for next-generation fuel-efficient aircraft

Growth in consumer electronics and sporting goods utilizing carbon fiber composites

Technological advancements improving carbon fiber cost-performance ratio

Potential Growth Constraints

High capital intensity and technical barriers for new precursor production facilities

Volatility in acrylonitrile monomer prices linked to propylene feedstock costs

Energy-intensive precursor oxidation and carbonization processes raising operational costs

Competition from alternative precursors like pitch-based or biomass-derived materials in niche applications

Geopolitical tensions and trade policies disrupting integrated global supply chains

Demand Structure by End-Use Industry

Carbon Fiber Production (for Wind Energy) (estimated share: 32%)

Wind energy represents the single largest and fastest-growing end-use for PAN-based carbon fiber, primarily for manufacturing turbine rotor blades. The current trend toward larger, more efficient offshore turbines necessitates longer blades that are impossible to produce without carbon fiber composites for stiffness and weight reduction. Through 2035, demand will be driven by global targets for renewable energy capacity, particularly in Asia-Pacific, Europe, and North America. The demand mechanism is direct: each new GW of installed wind capacity, especially offshore, translates into a quantifiable volume of carbon fiber, and consequently, PAN precursor. Key demand-side indicators include annual wind turbine installations, average rotor diameter (which is increasing), and the carbon fiber penetration rate in blade spar caps. The shift is from glass fiber-dominated designs to hybrid and full-carbon designs for blades exceeding 80 meters, locking in long-term precursor demand. Current trend: Rapid Growth.

Major trends: Transition to offshore wind farms with larger turbine capacities, Increase in average rotor diameter requiring advanced materials for structural integrity, Standardization of carbon fiber spar cap design in long blades, Development of recyclable thermoplastic composite blades influencing precursor research, and Vertical integration between precursor producers and wind blade manufacturers.

Representative participants: LM Wind Power (GE Vernova), Siemens Gamesa Renewable Energy, Vestas Wind Systems A/S, TPI Composites Inc, and Nordex SE.

Carbon Fiber Production (for Aerospace & Defense) (estimated share: 25%)

Aerospace remains a premium, high-value sector demanding the highest-grade PAN precursors for carbon fiber used in primary and secondary aircraft structures. Current demand is tied to production rates of next-generation aircraft like the Boeing 787 and Airbus A350, which utilize 50%+ composites by weight. Through 2035, growth will be supported by new aircraft programs, fleet renewal for fuel efficiency, and expanding use in urban air mobility and drone platforms. The demand mechanism is program-based and long-cycle, with precursor requirements locked in years ahead of aircraft delivery. Demand-side indicators include commercial aircraft order backlogs, defense budgets for advanced platforms, and the composite content per airframe in new designs. The evolution involves not just volume but also a push toward intermediate modulus and higher tensile strength fibers for next-gen structures, requiring precise precursor chemistry. Current trend: Steady Growth.

Major trends: Increased composite usage in single-aisle aircraft platforms, Development of sustainable aviation fuel-compatible composite materials, Growth in military UAV and advanced fighter jet programs, Research into out-of-autoclave processing to reduce manufacturing costs, and Focus on thermoplastic composites for repairability and recycling.

Representative participants: Airbus SE, The Boeing Company, Lockheed Martin Corporation, Spirit AeroSystems, and GKN Aerospace.

Carbon Fiber Production (for Automotive & Pressure Vessels) (estimated share: 18%)

This segment encompasses two synergistic drivers: automotive lightweighting for electric and internal combustion vehicles, and hydrogen storage/transport via Type IV composite pressure vessels. Current demand is emerging, with carbon fiber used in high-end vehicles and for compressed natural gas (CNG) tanks. The forecast to 2035 anticipates a tipping point as cost reductions and regulatory pressure converge. For automotive, the mechanism is weight savings to extend EV range, measured in kg of CFRP per vehicle. For pressure vessels, it's driven by the build-out of hydrogen refueling infrastructure for fuel cell vehicles and industrial storage, measured in the number of vessels and their capacity. Demand indicators include EV production volumes, corporate average fuel economy (CAFE) standards, hydrogen refueling station deployment, and policies supporting fuel cell vehicles. The shift is from niche to mass-market applications, demanding precursor innovations for higher-speed, lower-cost carbon fiber production. Current trend: Accelerating Growth.

Major trends: Adoption of carbon fiber in battery enclosures and structural components for EVs, Standardization of Type IV tank design for hydrogen across trucking and passenger vehicles, Development of high-volume, lower-cost carbon fiber processes for automotive, Integration of carbon fiber in fuel cell system components, and Growth in natural gas vehicle markets in certain regions sustaining CNG tank demand.

Representative participants: Toyota Motor Corporation, BMW Group, Hexagon Purus, ILJIN Composite, and Luxfer Holdings PLC.

Acrylic Fiber Production (estimated share: 20%)

PAN precursors for acrylic fiber represent the traditional, large-volume segment of the market, used in textiles, apparel, home furnishings, and industrial applications. Current demand is mature, with growth largely tied to population and GDP in emerging economies, while developed markets are stable or declining. Through 2035, this segment will see regional divergence: stagnation in North America and Europe, but sustained growth in Asia-Pacific, the Middle East, and Africa driven by urbanization and rising disposable incomes. The demand mechanism is fundamentally linked to textile and clothing production volumes. Key indicators include polyester-acrylic fiber price differentials, cotton production volatility, and fashion trends favoring acrylic blends for specific properties like bulk and color fastness. The evolution involves a focus on specialty acrylics for industrial applications like filtration and concrete reinforcement, which offer higher margins than standard textile grades. Current trend: Mature, Regionally Variable.

Major trends: Shift of acrylic fiber production capacity to Asia-Pacific and Turkey, Growing demand for acrylic fibers in outdoor and performance fabrics, Development of flame-retardant and anti-microbial acrylic variants, Replacement of wool with acrylic in cost-sensitive applications, and Use of acrylic fibers in wet-laid nonwovens for filtration media.

Representative participants: Aditya Birla Group, AKSA Akrilik Kimya Sanayii A.Ş, Dralon GmbH, Formosa Plastics Corporation, and Jilin Chemical Fiber Group.

Industrial Applications (Filtration, Construction, Others) (estimated share: 5%)

This segment covers diverse industrial uses of PAN precursors, primarily for producing precursor fibers used in wet-laid nonwovens for high-temperature filtration (e.g., coal-fired power plants, cement production) and as concrete reinforcement fibers. Current demand is specialized and driven by environmental regulations requiring advanced filtration and infrastructure needs. Through 2035, growth will be steady, supported by global infrastructure investment and tightening emissions standards. The demand mechanism is project-based and regulatory. For filtration, indicators include the number of operational coal plants with baghouse filters and regulations on particulate matter. For construction, it's linked to large-scale infrastructure projects in seismic zones or requiring high-durability concrete. The segment demands specific precursor properties, such as specific copolymer compositions for thermal stability, creating dedicated, high-value niches. Current trend: Niche Innovation.

Major trends: Stringent environmental regulations driving upgrades to industrial filtration systems, Use of PAN-based oxidized fibers in high-temperature gaskets and seals, Adoption of synthetic microfibers for concrete reinforcement in infrastructure, Growth in demand for specialty nonwovens in battery separators (using PAN derivatives), and Development of carbon felt from precursor fibers for insulation and composites.

Representative participants: Hollingsworth & Vose Company, Lydall, Inc. (part of Unifrax), Freudenberg Performance Materials, SGL Carbon SE, and BNZ Materials, Inc.

Key Market Participants

Regional Dynamics

Asia-Pacific (estimated share: 55%)

Asia-Pacific is the undisputed production and consumption leader, driven by China's massive carbon fiber capacity expansion, Japan's and South Korea's advanced precursor technology, and growing downstream demand from wind, automotive, and electronics. The region will see the highest growth rate, attracting significant investment in new integrated precursor-carbon fiber lines. Direction: Consolidating Dominance.

North America (estimated share: 20%)

North America's market is characterized by strong demand from aerospace (Boeing, defense) and a resurgence in industrial and wind energy applications. Growth is supported by policies like the Inflation Reduction Act, incentivizing clean energy and advanced manufacturing. The region remains a technology leader but faces competitive pressure from Asian imports on standard grades. Direction: Strategic Growth.

Europe (estimated share: 18%)

Europe's market is propelled by its ambitious Green Deal, creating robust demand for carbon fiber in wind energy, hydrogen infrastructure, and automotive lightweighting. The region boasts strong technical capabilities but faces high energy costs impacting precursor production economics. Strategic focus is on sustainability and circular economy initiatives within the composite value chain. Direction: Policy-Driven Transition.

Latin America (estimated share: 4%)

Latin America represents a smaller, emerging market with growth pockets in acrylic fiber production and nascent wind energy projects. Brazil and Mexico are key consumers. The market is constrained by limited local precursor production, relying on imports, but holds potential as a future location for cost-competitive manufacturing. Direction: Emerging Niche.

Middle East & Africa (estimated share: 3%)

This region is currently a minor player but holds strategic interest due to access to petrochemical feedstocks (acrylonitrile) and growing investments in downstream industries. Saudi Arabia and Turkey are focal points. Growth will be gradual, initially serving local acrylic fiber demand before potentially developing carbon fiber capabilities aligned with economic diversification plans. Direction: Developing Potential.

Market Outlook (2026-2035)

In the baseline scenario, IndexBox estimates a 8.2% compound annual growth rate for the global polyacrylonitrile precursors market over 2026-2035, bringing the market index to roughly 220 by 2035 (2025=100).

Note: indexed curves are used to compare medium-term scenario trajectories when full absolute volumes are not publicly disclosed.

title: SAF Bill Introduced in St. Paul | Red River Farm Network
description:
A bill has been introduced in the Minnesota House that would impose a moratorium on sustainable aviation fuel tax credits until the Environmental Quality Board establishes a generic environmental impact statement for new facilities.
House Agriculture Committee co-chair Rick Hansen and Tax Committee co-chair Aisha Gomez introduced the bill.
If passed, this proposal would result in a five-year ban on the construction of new SAF facilities in the state.

title: Stay vigilant on Middle East tensions, MATTA tells travellers - The Star
description:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Association of Tour and Travel Agents (MATTA) has advised travellers to remain vigilant and monitor flight status updates following international flight disruptions caused by tensions in the Middle East.
MATTA vice-president (air transportation) Dr Fazil Khan said conflict in the region has forced airlines to implement flight suspensions, rerouting and schedule adjustments as precautionary measures.
"Several major aviation hubs and airspaces in the Middle East, including those in the Gulf and neighbouring regions, are currently operating under enhanced security measures.
"MATTA members will, as always, extend their fullest cooperation and professionalism in assisting clients to explore alternative arrangements where possible.
However, such alternatives remain subject to the pre-determined commercial policies and operational decisions of airlines and hotel partners on the ground," he said.
text:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Association of Tour and Travel Agents (MATTA) has advised travellers to remain vigilant and monitor flight status updates following international flight disruptions caused by tensions in the Middle East.

MATTA vice-president (air transportation) Dr Fazil Khan said conflict in the region has forced airlines to implement flight suspensions, rerouting and schedule adjustments as precautionary measures.

"Several major aviation hubs and airspaces in the Middle East, including those in the Gulf and neighbouring regions, are currently operating under enhanced security measures.

"These developments have led to delays, cancellations and temporary airspace restrictions affecting flights transiting through or operating to and from the region," he said in a statement on Sunday (March 1).

Fazil noted that the situation remains fluid and may change at short notice depending on security assessments by regional authorities and airlines.

He further clarified that these disruptions are beyond the control of travel agents, as operational decisions regarding flight changes are made solely by airlines and aviation authorities, based on security and safety considerations.

"Any changes to travel arrangements, including re-accommodation, refunds, or itinerary amendments, are strictly subject to the respective terms and conditions imposed by airlines and service providers.

"MATTA members will, as always, extend their fullest cooperation and professionalism in assisting clients to explore alternative arrangements where possible. However, such alternatives remain subject to the pre-determined commercial policies and operational decisions of airlines and hotel partners on the ground," he said. - Bernama

title: How a landfill waste-to-energy project gave way to a Rockford data center
description:
By Kevin Haas Rock River Current Get our mobile app ROCKFORD — Landfills initially helped bring data center developer Monarch Energy to town.
The Trump administration rolled back much of those clean energy incentives.
Data centers have also caused energy prices to surge in many communities where they take root.
Powering a data center can use substantial amounts of electricity, and cooling the rows of server stacks can consume extensive amounts of water.
“Most of our executive team, our backgrounds, really do come from clean energy and the sustainability space.
text:
By Kevin Haas

Rock River Current

Get our mobile app

ROCKFORD — Landfills initially helped bring data center developer Monarch Energy to town.

The San Diego-based infrastructure developer was attracted to land south of the Chicago Rockford International Airport in part because of its proximity to two landfills: the Winnebago Landfill just south of Baxter Road and the Orchard Hills Landfill just west of Illinois Route 251.

Monarch, which at the time was focused on green hydrogen production, had plans to use carbon dioxide byproduct from the decomposition of trash to produce sustainable aviation fuel for customers at the Chicago Rockford International Airport and beyond.

Air Liquide Advanced Technologies has a similar waste-to-energy facility that uses methane gas from the Winnebago Landfill. Monarch was preparing to do the same with carbon dioxide. The company was here in April 2024 with U.S. Sen. Tammy Duckworth and a multitude of local, state and federal officials to talk about its potential $1 billion investment to produce sustainable aviation fuel.

But the capital stack for that project was in part reliant on tax credits made available through the Inflation Reduction Act, which provided incentives for clean hydrogen production. The Trump administration rolled back much of those clean energy incentives. So Monarch pivoted to focus on highly demanded data centers.

“Initially our intent was in fact to make clean hydrogen. We did the work to secure power and secure water and permits and all the entitlements needed for a large industrial project, but over time the winds of change came along and there were changes of legislation that are outside of our control,” said Ben Alingh, CEO of Monarch Energy. “But the fundamentals remain the same where we had a great site … So we can look to other uses of those fundamentals.”

$12B investment

Now, Monarch is preparing for a potential investment of up to $12 billion to create a Rockford data center on hundreds of acres of land south of the airport. The center is expected to create 200 technology jobs in addition to more than 1,000 construction jobs. It would also generate millions in property tax revenue for the area, with an initial rough estimate at close to $60 million annually, according to said Michael Dunn Jr., executive director of R1, which has been working with Monarch for years on development in the area.

“We believe it will be one of the largest developments, if not the largest developments in our city’s history,” Mayor Tom McNamara said. “It’s another example that our area is progressing and our momentum is catching wind behind its sails. We are seeing more and more businesses wanting to come to Rockford.”

McNamara said bringing Monarch here has been a multi-year coordinated effort between the city, county, state representatives, federal officials, Gov. JB Pritzker’s office and the Illinois Department of Commerce and Economic Opportunity, among others. That effort started with a clean aviation energy goal.

“This has genuinely been one of those times that all units of government have been working together and we’ve been doing so now for years,” he said. “I’m now on weekly phone calls and having weekly meetings about our ability to land this development.”

The company that the data center would serve (i.e. Meta, Amazon, Google, Microsoft) has not yet been named.

Monarch said it hopes to be able to announce its user soon. Its goal is to start construction in 2027 and be in operation by 2028, pending state and local approvals.

“This is advanced technology. This is infrastructure investment. These are jobs that cannot be outsourced,” Alingh said. “This is extremely attractive tax revenue that can help keep local tax rates down over time while helping fund the community’s needs.”

Dunn said that although Monarch Energy has shifted its focus to data centers, there is plenty of land available south of the airport for a waste-to-energy project if momentum or policy shift back that way.

“I don’t think that (the wasteo-to-energy) plant is canceled. I think that plant is delayed. There’s enough land there to always accommodate it,” Dunn said. “There’s no appetite for it on the federal level right now, but that doesn’t mean that appetite doesn’t come back.”

Resource-hungry development

Data center developments come with concerns over the massive usage of water and electricity, as well as potential air and water pollution. Data centers have also caused energy prices to surge in many communities where they take root. President Trump addressed the issue in his State of the Union speech, pledging to make AI data centers responsible for their own power supply to prevent residents’ bills from skyrocketing.

The concern over data centers has been growing as the AI boom has fueled the development of more and more facilities. Powering a data center can use substantial amounts of electricity, and cooling the rows of server stacks can consume extensive amounts of water.

Dunn said Monarch’s background as a clean energy company positions it to address environmental and resource consumption concerns.

“We believe that Monarch is a good development partner because of their pedigree in being a clean energy developer,” Dunn said. “They’re not a data center developer. They’re a clean energy developer. We have a partner with Monarch that has a background and track record of doing environmentally friendly infrastructure.”

Alingh said that Monarch will strive to be as responsible and efficient as possible.

“We believe in responsible development and water availability in any region is a super important issue, and being as sustainable as possible in any of our water use intentions is really core to our identity,” he said. “Most of our executive team, our backgrounds, really do come from clean energy and the sustainability space. We’re hyper-focused on that. We know that communities are concerned about these issues and concerned about the environmental impacts. At the same time these projects are a part of our society and an important piece of the economy.”

Dunn said many of the national horror stories over data center resource consumption have come from hot-climate states, where there are fewer months of cold temperatures to help cool the systems.

“If you have a data center that can only have cool air outside it that it can bring in six weeks of the year, it’s going to use up a lot of water,” Dunn said. “If you looking at the data centers that have been proposed in the suburbs, those data centers are planning to bring 10 months of cool air from outside into the building, versus using mechanical equipment.”

He said estimates on energy usage here will be more refined once we know Monarch’s end user.

“This isn’t a done deal,” Dunn said. “Whoever does come here has to prove to us that they’re going to be responsible with power and water.”

title: KLIA running smoothly despite several flight cancellations, says operator - The Star
description:
SEPANG: Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) operations remain stable and orderly despite flight cancellations linked to the Middle East conflict, according to Malaysia Airports Holdings Bhd (MAHB).
The airport operator said a total of 26 flights have been cancelled, comprising 13 departures and 13 arrivals.
"These involved selected services to and from affected Middle East destinations and certain long-haul routings that require airspace adjustments," it said.
Iran has carried out retaliatory missile attacks on Israeli territory as well as on US military facilities in the Middle East.
Major carriers, including Malaysia Airlines, Qatar Airways, Saudia, and Abu Dhabi-based airlines, have suspended or cancelled multiple flights since yesterday.
text:
SEPANG: Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA) operations remain stable and orderly despite flight cancellations linked to the Middle East conflict, according to Malaysia Airports Holdings Bhd (MAHB).

The airport operator said both KLIA Terminal 1 and Terminal 2 continue to operate fully, with passenger processing at check-in, security screening and immigration proceeding without disruption.

The airport operator said a total of 26 flights have been cancelled, comprising 13 departures and 13 arrivals.

"These involved selected services to and from affected Middle East destinations and certain long-haul routings that require airspace adjustments," it said.

At this time, MAHB confirmed that no stranded passengers have been registered under its Crisis Response System, with passenger flow across both terminals remaining well managed.

However, MAHB cautioned that the situation remains fluid and subject to airspace and airline safety assessments, with further schedule adjustments highly likely.

"Airlines are continuously monitoring airspace developments, particularly those operating long-haul routes, as alternative routing may be needed to avoid affected zones.

"Malaysia Airports will continue to monitor developments closely and lead coordinated response efforts to ensure safe, orderly and efficient airport operations," it said.

Passengers travelling to or transiting through impacted regions are strongly advised to check their flight status directly with their airline before heading to the airport, ensure their contact details are updated, and monitor official airline communications for the latest updates.

According to MAHB, regular crisis coordination meetings are being convened, with situation reports received from key stakeholders to ensure a consolidated and up-to-date operational picture.

It noted that airlines are required to notify airport management of any operational revisions, including cancellations, diversions or schedule adjustments.

"Contingency measures remain in place should the situation escalate, with the airport community operating under a structured and coordinated response mechanism," MAHB said.

The United States and Israel launched a series of attacks on targets in Iran on Saturday, including Tehran.

Iran has carried out retaliatory missile attacks on Israeli territory as well as on US military facilities in the Middle East.

Thousands of Middle Eastern flights have been cancelled, and airspace in the region has been closed following the development.

Major carriers, including Malaysia Airlines, Qatar Airways, Saudia, and Abu Dhabi-based airlines, have suspended or cancelled multiple flights since yesterday. - Bernama

title: How Kenya Airways Shapes Kenya's Ksh 425 Billion Aviation Industry - Soko Directory
description:
And at the center of that infrastructure sits Kenya Airways (KQ).
Aviation as Economic Infrastructure In 2024, Kenya Airways recorded KSh 188.4 billion in revenue, cementing its position as one of Kenya’s key foreign exchange contributors.
In today’s global economy, foreign currency stability is not a footnote — it is survival.
Nairobi: A Continental Hub by Design In 2024, 5.23 million passengers moved through Kenya Airways’ network.
More Than a Balance Sheet Kenya Airways is often evaluated through profitability metrics — as any corporate entity should be.
text:
When we talk about aviation in Kenya, we often think about boarding passes, departures screens, and the familiar red, green, and black tail of Kenya Airways lifting into the sky. But behind every takeoff is something far bigger than travel.

Kenya’s aviation sector contributes KSh 425 billion annually to the economy — that’s 3.1% of GDP. Those numbers are not just impressive; they are structural. Aviation in Kenya is not a luxury industry. It is economic infrastructure.

And at the center of that infrastructure sits Kenya Airways (KQ).

Aviation as Economic Infrastructure

In 2024, Kenya Airways recorded KSh 188.4 billion in revenue, cementing its position as one of Kenya’s key foreign exchange contributors. In today’s global economy, foreign currency stability is not a footnote — it is survival. Airlines like KQ are not just transporting people; they are transporting value into the country.

Across aviation, tourism, and supply chains, the sector supports approximately 460,000 jobs. That means when we speak about Kenya Airways, we are really speaking about livelihoods — from pilots and cabin crew to farmers, hotel workers, cargo handlers, and thousands of small business owners who rely on connectivity.

This is the multiplier effect in motion.

Nairobi: A Continental Hub by Design

In 2024, 5.23 million passengers moved through Kenya Airways’ network. That is 5.23 million individual touchpoints for tourism, trade, conferences, diaspora travel, and investment — all converging through Nairobi.

With 46 global destinations, including 37 African routes, Nairobi’s position as a continental aviation hub did not happen by accident. It was built strategically. It was built consistently. And it is sustained daily by the operations of Kenya Airways.

Every flight is a corridor of commerce.

The impact is visible in tourism alone. Aviation travel supports approximately $1.2 billion in tourism revenue. Hotels, tour operators, restaurants, parks, and conference centers depend on reliable air connectivity. KQ is often the silent enabler of these ecosystems.

Connecting Farms to Foreign Exchange

The true power of aviation becomes even clearer when we look at cargo.

In 2024, Kenya Airways moved 70,776 tonnes of cargo, ensuring Kenyan exports reach global markets quickly and efficiently. Aviation is especially critical for perishables and high-value goods — products that cannot afford delay.

Despite global economic pressures, KQ achieved 25% cargo growth. That growth is more than an operational win; it is an indicator of export resilience.

Behind those cargo volumes are more than 200,000 horticulture jobs. Flowers, fresh produce, and specialty agricultural products rely on rapid export channels to remain competitive in international markets. Aviation quite literally connects Kenyan farms to foreign exchange earnings.

Without reliable air cargo, entire value chains would slow down.

The Multiplier Effect at Home

Kenya Airways also plays a powerful domestic economic role.

With 35% local procurement, the airline channels significant spending directly into Kenyan businesses. From catering to engineering services, fuel supply to logistics, the airline’s ecosystem extends far beyond the runway.

More than 10,000 local suppliers are powered through KQ’s operational network. Large corporations create multiplier effects beyond their balance sheets. When KQ spends, thousands of businesses benefit. When it grows, suppliers grow.

This is how anchor institutions shape economies.

Sustainability in Motion

Modern aviation must balance growth with responsibility — and Kenya Airways is making measurable shifts.

KQ conducted the first intra-Africa Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) flight, marking a significant milestone for cleaner aviation on the continent. This is not symbolic; it signals Africa’s readiness to participate in global decarbonization efforts.

Operationally, 12% of KQ’s ground operations are powered by renewable energy. That represents a tangible shift toward sustainable infrastructure rather than sustainability rhetoric.

Even incremental improvements matter at scale. Through catering initiatives, KQ has reduced 24 tonnes of plastic annually. In an industry that operates at massive volumes, small efficiencies compound into significant environmental gains.

Inclusion in the Skies

Progress is not only environmental; it is social.

Of Kenya Airways’ 5,000-strong workforce, 44% are women. In a sector historically dominated by men, this level of representation signals meaningful progress toward inclusive aviation leadership in Africa.

Diversity in aviation is not cosmetic. It strengthens decision-making, innovation, and corporate culture.

More Than a Balance Sheet

Kenya Airways is often evaluated through profitability metrics — as any corporate entity should be. But to view it purely through quarterly numbers is to miss the broader story.

This is an airline that:

Supports nearly half a million jobs across connected sectors

Moves millions of passengers annually

Channels billions in revenue into the economy

Connects farms to foreign exchange markets

Sustains thousands of local suppliers

Drives tourism and regional integration

Advances in sustainability and inclusion in African aviation

Aviation is not just about getting from point A to point B. It is about linking markets, cultures, and opportunity.

In many ways, Kenya Airways is not just flying aircraft. It is flying economic corridors. It is flying livelihoods. It is flying Kenya’s connectivity to the world.

And in today’s interconnected global economy, that may be one of the most important routes of all.

title: Otvoreno gradilište velikog industrijskog pogona: Proizvodit će ono bez čega avioni ...
description:
U industrijskoj zoni Delfzijla započela je izgradnja prve europske tvornice namijenjene proizvodnji održivog avionskog goriva (SAF – Sustainable Aviation Fuel), čime Europa dobiva jedan od ključnih projekata energetske tranzicije u zrakoplovstvu.
Projekt razvija nizozemska tvrtka SkyNRG, a riječ je o prvom postrojenju takve vrste na europskom tlu koje ulazi u punu fazu realizacije.
Riječ je o investiciji koja spaja visoku tehnologiju, složenu procesnu industriju i napredna građevinska rješenja.
Za građevinski sektor riječ je o investiciji koja objedinjuje znanja iz klasične industrijske gradnje, procesnog inženjeringa i energetike.
Strateški projekt Izgradnja tvornice dolazi u trenutku kada Europska unija postavlja ambiciozne ciljeve za smanjenje emisija stakleničkih plinova u prometu, osobito u zrakoplovstvu.
text:
U industrijskoj zoni Delfzijla započela je izgradnja prve europske tvornice namijenjene proizvodnji održivog avionskog goriva (SAF – Sustainable Aviation Fuel), čime Europa dobiva jedan od ključnih projekata energetske tranzicije u zrakoplovstvu. Projekt razvija nizozemska tvrtka SkyNRG, a riječ je o prvom postrojenju takve vrste na europskom tlu koje ulazi u punu fazu realizacije.

Riječ je o investiciji koja spaja visoku tehnologiju, složenu procesnu industriju i napredna građevinska rješenja. Projekt se ističe ne samo zbog klimatskog značaja, već i zbog opsega radova i razine inženjerske kompleksnosti. Ujedno potvrđuje sve snažniji trend ulaganja u infrastrukturu povezanu s dekarbonizacijom prometa. Za građevinski sektor to je još jedan pokazatelj kako će zelena tranzicija generirati novu generaciju velikih industrijskih gradnji.

Futurističko proširenje i redizajn domaće zračne luke: Čeličnu 'saće' konstrukciju izradila dva softwera

Također, investicija predstavlja važan iskorak u smjeru dekarbonizacije zračnog prometa, ali i značajan građevinski zahvat visoke tehnološke složenosti. Planirano je da postrojenje nakon dovršetka proizvodi oko 100.000 tona SAF-a godišnje.

Početak komercijalne proizvodnje očekuje se u godinama nakon završetka izgradnje i testiranja sustava. Projekt se promatra i kao model budućih industrijskih investicija u sektoru obnovljivih goriva diljem Europe.

Kompleksan industrijski zahvat

Prema navodima iz tvrtke SkyNR, tvornica se gradi kao integrirani industrijski kompleks koji uključuje procesna postrojenja, spremnike, logističku infrastrukturu te sustave za energetsku i komunalnu podršku. Riječ je o greenfield investiciji koja zahtijeva opsežne pripremne radove, temeljne konstrukcije za tešku procesnu opremu te razvoj interne prometne i cjevovodne mreže,

Poseban izazov predstavlja ugradnja reaktorskih jedinica i sustava za preradu sirovina na bazi otpadnih ulja i masti, koji se koriste za proizvodnju goriva putem HEFA tehnologije. Projekt uključuje i izgradnju skladišnih kapaciteta te priključaka na postojeću logističku infrastrukturu luke i željeznice.

Renomirani izvođač

Kao inženjerski i izvođački partner angažirana je međunarodna kompanija Technip Energies, specijalizirana za kompleksna energetska i procesna postrojenja. Time je naglašena visoka razina tehničkih zahtjeva i potreba za preciznom koordinacijom projektiranja, nabave i izvođenja radova.

Ovakvi projekti podrazumijevaju stroge sigurnosne i okolišne standarde, kao i napredne sustave nadzora i automatizacije. Za građevinski sektor riječ je o investiciji koja objedinjuje znanja iz klasične industrijske gradnje, procesnog inženjeringa i energetike.

Strateški projekt

Izgradnja tvornice dolazi u trenutku kada Europska unija postavlja ambiciozne ciljeve za smanjenje emisija stakleničkih plinova u prometu, osobito u zrakoplovstvu. Naime, SAF se smatra jednim od ključnih rješenja jer omogućuje značajno smanjenje emisija u usporedbi s konvencionalnim kerozinom, bez potrebe za velikim izmjenama postojeće zrakoplovne flote. Procjene pokazuju da se korištenjem ovakvog goriva emisije tijekom životnog ciklusa mogu smanjiti i do 80 posto, ovisno o vrsti sirovine i energetskom miksu proizvodnje.

Europski velegrad dobit će novi aerodrom: U antičkom duhu zasjat će svjetlosne kupole

Nizozemska se ovim projektom dodatno pozicionira kao jedno od europskih središta razvoja održivih goriva i energetske infrastrukture. Industrijska zona u Delfzijlu već raspolaže razvijenom energetskom i logističkom mrežom, što je omogućilo relativno brzu pripremu projekta. Osim doprinosa klimatskim ciljevima, projekt ima i snažan gospodarski učinak kroz otvaranje radnih mjesta u fazi gradnje i kasnijeg operativnog rada. Ujedno se očekuje da će potaknuti razvoj sličnih postrojenja u drugim dijelovima Europe.

text:
In Germany, International Sustainability and Carbon Certification (ISCC) has partnered with OMV and Airbus to support the further development of the global SAF ecosystem.

This collaboration is grounded in a shared focus on Book and Claim Chain-of-Custody models. Each partner brings a distinct perspective: energy production, aircraft manufacturing and operation, and sustainability certification, according to ISCC.

The partners said they are exploring how Book and Claim approaches can help scale SAF while maintaining credibility, transparency and environmental integrity.

As part of this pilot project, Airbus will take the role of facilitator in the ISCC Credit Transfer System. This will involve facilitating transactions between supply chain actors, such as airlines and corporate partners. In adding this new role, we aim to support cohesion and trust across global aviation value chains.

title: Swedavia expands joint sustainable aviation fuel purchase : The Daily Digest
description:
In India, BioEnergy Times reported that Sweden’s state-owned airport operator, Swedavia, has increased its annual joint purchase of sustainable aviation fuel to reduce emissions from business travel.
This year, the program includes new participants such as Luleå Municipality and the ground handling company Aviator Airport Alliance.
Swedavia said it is especially important that Luleå Municipality has joined, as this is the first time a Swedish local authority has participated in the scheme.
The company said the move shows that emissions from air travel can already be reduced by choosing cleaner fuel, the report added.
In the 2025 purchasing round, a total of 300 tons of sustainable aviation fuel were ordered, including 215 tons bought on behalf of Swedavia itself.
text:
In India, BioEnergy Times reported that Sweden’s state-owned airport operator, Swedavia, has increased its annual joint purchase of sustainable aviation fuel to reduce emissions from business travel.

This year, the program includes new participants such as Luleå Municipality and the ground handling company Aviator Airport Alliance. Emergency services provider SOS Alarm has also taken part for the sixth year in a row, according to the report.

Swedavia said it is especially important that Luleå Municipality has joined, as this is the first time a Swedish local authority has participated in the scheme. The company said the move shows that emissions from air travel can already be reduced by choosing cleaner fuel, the report added.

In the 2025 purchasing round, a total of 300 tons of sustainable aviation fuel were ordered, including 215 tons bought on behalf of Swedavia itself.

title: How Modern Airport Security Systems Are Redefining Passenger Safety in 2026
description:
Modern airport security systems are now built around AI-powered surveillance, biometric identity verification, real-time analytics, and integrated command centers.
In 2026, airport security systems are redefining passenger safety by being predictive instead of reactive, centralized instead of fragmented, and intelligent instead of passive.
For example, some modern airport security systems, including platforms such as Coram’s airport security system, connect existing IP cameras into a unified cloud-managed platform.
Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Protection Modern airport security systems are highly connected.
Why This Matters for Travelers For passengers in 2026, modern airport security systems mean faster processing and reduced wait times for passengers.
text:
Air travel has never been safer, yet threats have never been more complex. Airports in 2026 face a dual challenge: increasing passenger volume and evolving security risks. According to the International Air Transport Association (IATA), global passenger traffic is projected to exceed 4.7 billion travelers annually, surpassing pre-pandemic levels.

At the same time, aviation authorities report rising cybersecurity incidents, insider threats, and perimeter breaches worldwide.

Most airports were originally designed for a different era. Traditional CCTV, manual ID checks, and reactive security protocols are no longer sufficient.

Modern airport security systems are now built around AI-powered surveillance, biometric identity verification, real-time analytics, and integrated command centers.

The result is a transformation that goes far beyond metal detectors and baggage scanners. In 2026, airport security systems are redefining passenger safety by being predictive instead of reactive, centralized instead of fragmented, and intelligent instead of passive.

This article breaks down exactly how.

The Problem: More Passengers, Higher Risk, Greater Complexity

Airports today function as mini-cities. Major hubs like Atlanta, Dubai, and London Heathrow process over 60 to 100 million passengers annually. Each terminal includes retail zones, baggage systems, restricted staff areas, and aircraft operations—all requiring constant monitoring.

The security challenge includes:

Physical threats such as unauthorized access and perimeter intrusions

Insider risks from credential misuse

Cyber vulnerabilities in connected infrastructure

Crowd management during peak travel seasons

Compliance with global aviation safety standards

According to the U.S. Transportation Security Administration (TSA), firearms detected at airport checkpoints reached record levels in recent years, underscoring the importance of layered detection systems.

The traditional model—where cameras simply recorded footage for later review—cannot keep up. Modern airports need systems that analyze, alert, and respond in real time.

1. AI-Powered Video Surveillance: From Passive Recording to Active Detection

One of the most significant shifts in 2026 is the move from legacy CCTV to AI-powered video surveillance systems.

What’s Different?

Instead of security teams manually watching dozens of screens, AI algorithms now:

Detect unusual behavior patterns

Identify abandoned objects

Recognize perimeter breaches

Flag restricted-area access attempts

Reduce false alarms by distinguishing real threats from routine activity

According to research from MarketsandMarkets, the AI in video surveillance market is growing at over 20% CAGR, reflecting strong global adoption in aviation and transportation.

Why It Matters for Passengers

For travelers, this means faster response times and fewer security gaps. For example:

Suspicious activity in a terminal can trigger an alert within seconds.

Unauthorized access to an aircraft maintenance zone can immediately notify operations teams.

Crowd congestion can be monitored to prevent dangerous bottlenecks.

The shift is clear: airports are no longer reviewing footage after incidents—they are preventing incidents before escalation.

Airports worldwide are adopting biometric technology to reduce friction while maintaining security.

Real-World Adoption

U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) reports biometric facial comparison systems are deployed at over 200 U.S. airports.

The European Union is expanding biometric entry-exit systems to streamline border control.

Dubai International Airport uses smart gates with biometric verification to process passengers in seconds.

Benefits

Reduced wait times

Improved identity verification accuracy

Lower reliance on manual document inspection

Enhanced detection of fraudulent travel documents

Importantly, biometric systems in aviation are regulated under strict privacy guidelines, including data minimization and secure storage requirements.

3. Integrated Access Control and Perimeter Security

Airports contain thousands of restricted areas, including:

Runways

Baggage handling zones

Control towers

Fuel storage facilities

Maintenance hangars

Modern airport security systems integrate access control with surveillance and analytics.

What Integration Looks Like

When a badge is used to enter a secure area, the access control system logs the event. A linked camera feed then confirms visual identity. AI verifies whether the behavior matches normal activity.

Following this process, an alert is then generated if something is unusual.

This layered approach reduces insider risk, one of the aviation industry’s growing concerns.

4. Real-Time Command Centers: Centralized Visibility

Large airports now operate centralized command centers that unify:

Video surveillance

Access control

Environmental sensors

Emergency management systems

Security teams can monitor operations across terminals and airfields through a single dashboard.

For example, some modern airport security systems, including platforms such as Coram’s airport security system, connect existing IP cameras into a unified cloud-managed platform.

This allows airport authorities to centralize video feeds, link door access events to live footage, and receive intelligent alerts across multiple terminals without replacing every camera.

The focus is on real-time situational awareness and faster coordination during incidents.

This model reflects a broader industry trend: moving from siloed systems to integrated security ecosystems.

5. Smart Environmental Monitoring and Sensor Integration

Security in 2026 extends beyond cameras and ID checks.

Airports now deploy:

Gun detection systems

Smoke and fire sensors

Motion detectors

Intrusion alarms

Drone detection technologies

These systems feed data into AI platforms capable of correlating multiple signals.

For example, if a perimeter fence sensor detects movement and a nearby camera confirms activity, alerts escalate automatically.

If unusual heat signatures appear near restricted zones, security teams receive immediate notification. The goal is early detection rather than reactive response.

6. Crowd Analytics and Passenger Flow Optimization

Passenger experience and security are no longer separate goals.

Using AI-based crowd analytics, airports can:

Predict congestion at checkpoints

Adjust staffing dynamically

Prevent overcrowding in terminals

Identify unusual crowd movement patterns

ACI World expects passenger traffic to grow by more than 3–4% annually in the coming years. This makes crowd intelligence essential for safety and comfort.

7. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Protection

Modern airport security systems are highly connected. Cameras, sensors, badge readers, and databases are all networked.

This creates new cybersecurity challenges.

In 2022, several airports worldwide reported cyber incidents affecting operations. As a result, aviation authorities now emphasize:

End-to-end encryption

Multi-factor authentication

Segmented networks

Continuous monitoring

Security is no longer just physical—it is digital and operational.

8. Compliance and Global Aviation Standards

Airports must comply with a range of regulatory bodies, including TSA regulations (U.S.), ICAO security standards, GDPR (EU) privacy regulations, and local aviation authority rules

Modern systems are designed with compliance built in:

Audit logs

Controlled access permissions

Retention policies

Secure cloud infrastructure

Compliance is no longer a checkbox—it is embedded in system design.

Why This Matters for Travelers

For passengers in 2026, modern airport security systems mean faster processing and reduced wait times for passengers.

For airport authorities this translates into higher safety standards and better emergency response. Overall, it results in more predictable travel experiences. Security becomes less visible yet more effective.

The goal is clear: make safety seamless.

Key Takeaways

Global passenger traffic exceeds 4.7 billion annually, increasing security complexity

AI-powered surveillance reduces response times and improves threat detection

Biometric screening is now deployed in over 200 U.S. airports

Integrated access control reduces insider threats

Real-time command centers centralize visibility across terminals

Environmental sensors enhance perimeter protection

Cybersecurity is now a core part of airport security systems

The Future of Airport Security

Airport security in 2026 is not defined by checkpoints alone. It is defined by:

Intelligence

Integration

Speed

Compliance

Scalability

As travel continues to grow, security systems must evolve alongside it. The airports investing in unified, AI-driven platforms today are better prepared for tomorrow’s risks.

The real transformation is not just in technology—it is in philosophy. Security is no longer about reacting to incidents. It is about anticipating them, minimizing them, and responding in seconds when they occur.

Final Thought

Passenger safety is no longer just about physical barriers and manual inspections. It is about data, intelligence, and integration working together behind the scenes.

As airports continue modernizing, the question becomes: What level of security and seamless experience should passengers expect in the next decade of global travel?
